title: Matching and Pricing in Ride Hailing: Wild Goose Chases and How to Solve Them
abstract: We show that ride-hailing markets are prone to a matching failure (“wild goose chases”) in which high demand sets off a harmful feedback cycle of few idle drivers, high pickup times, and low earnings, drastically reducing welfare. After characterizing these failures theoretically and showing empirical evidence of their relevance, we analyze how platforms can avoid them. Raising prices brings demand back under control. Platforms can thus set a uniform high price, or they can use high “surge” pricing during high demand times while keeping prices low at other times. Some adjustments to the matching algorithm can also avoid the problem, but surge pricing performs better than them.

author.Juan Camilo Castillo: Department of Economics, University of Pennsylvania, Philadelphia, Pennsylvania 19130
author.Dan Knoepfle: Uber Technologies, Inc., San Francisco, California 94158
author.E. Glen Weyl: Plural Technology Collaboratory, Microsoft Research, Redmond, Washington 98005

article.1. Introduction: Ride-hailing platforms like Uber and Lyft introduced several innovations that improve on traditional taxis.Cramer and Krueger (2016)show that the fraction of working time that a driver spends with a rider in the back seat is roughly 40% higher for Uber than for taxis. Efficiency gains are possible, among other reasons, because of better technology that matches riders and drivers through smartphone location. Matching in ride hailing, however, is not more efficient than in taxi markets under all circumstances. We show in this paper that, unlike traditional street-hail taxi systems, the matching technology used by ride-hailing platforms is prone to a failure mode that drastically harms the market.1 When drivers are scarce relative to demand, drivers are quickly occupied, and idle drivers are thus spread thinly throughout a city. If the platform uses the widely usedfirst dispatchmatching protocol, in which every rider requesting a trip is matched immediately to the nearest idle driver, riders are matched to drivers who, on average, are far away. Riders are hurt because they must wait a long time before being picked up, but so, too, are drivers who, sent to pick up distant customers, waste time and suffer reduced earnings. This decreases the number of available drivers both directly by occupying cars and indirectly as drivers exit in the face of reduced earnings, exacerbating the problem. This harmful feedback cycle induces a failure mode—which we call awild goose chase(WGC), because drivers are unnecessarily sent far away to pick up riders—that can cause the market to collapse with a steep decline in welfare. Street-hail taxis, in contrast, do not enter WGCs. Drivers spend time roaming the streets looking for riders, but when demand is high, they find and pick up riders quickly, which avoids the harmful feedback cycle.2 In this paper, we show that ride-hailing markets can enter into WGCs, and we analyze what platforms can do to avoid them. We set up a model of a ride-hailing platform that matches through first dispatch. When demand is high relative to supply, the market falls into a WGC. We find empirical evidence that WGCs occur in the data, which poses a market design challenge: successful platforms must set in place mechanisms to avoid WGCs. We analyze the options available to platforms. Many such platforms, including Uber and Lyft, rely primarily on surge pricing: they raise prices when a WGC would have otherwise occurred, tackling the excess demand that causes WGCs. Platforms can also make certain changes to the dispatch protocol. We analyze some of these changes and show that surge pricing performs better than them, which might explain why platforms use surge pricing despite its negative reputation. Our analysis starts with a simple static/steady-state theoretical model in homogeneous space. Rider demand decreases with price and pickup time, and drivers’ labor supply increases with per-unit-time earnings. Riders who request a trip are matched to drivers according to first dispatch. The platform chooses the price level, and then the market, as in taxi markets, clears through times: The equilibrium pickup time is the value such that demand equals supply. The key feature that gives rise to WGCs is that thetrip supply, defined as the number of trips that a fixed number of drivers can provide, is a nonmonotone function of the pickup time. It is increasing when the pickup time is low. If more idle drivers are waiting to be matched, the nearest driver is, on average, closer and the pickup time is lower. Thus, a higher pickup time requires fewer idle drivers, which frees up drivers who can supply more trips. However, an opposing effect kicks in as pickup times rise. Drivers spend more time en route to pick up passengers, reducing the number of trips they can complete. This second effect eventually dominates: the trip supply reaches a maximum and then decreases. In contrast, with street-hail taxis, the trip supply is monotonically increasing: Drivers spend no time picking up passengers after finding them on the street, and so the second effect is not present. We say a WGC occurs when trip supply is in the decreasing region. In that case, unusual comparative statics arise: Increasing demand decreases the number of trips, setting off a harmful feedback cycle of lower driver earnings, lower labor supply, and even fewer trips. Ultimately, a sharp decrease in welfare occurs. We show that WGCs are easy to diagnose empirically: they occur when the ratio of idle drivers to those on the way to pick up a customer—what we callslack—falls below a critical threshold (between 0.25 and 0.45, depending on road and traffic patterns). We back our theoretical findings with an empirical analysis of Uber in Manhattan. We show that trip supply is indeed nonmonotonic in pickup times. Furthermore, the data bears out our prediction of sharp decay in the market.Figure 1shows that rider cancellations, a simple measure of market health, increase drastically when we predict that WGCs take place. These are precisely the times when Uber sets high prices, suggesting that it uses surge pricing to avoid WGCs. Having thus validated the empirical relevance of WGCs, we then analyze what platforms can do to avoid them. Rather than looking for optimal mechanisms—a dynamic programming problem that requires forecasting both demand and supply and keeping track of the location of every rider and driver—we focus on simple pricing rules and changes to the matching protocol. We first analyze pricing. When the market is in a WGC, increasing prices increases welfare and it eventually takes the market out of the WGC. This suggests two market-wide policies. First, one can set a sufficiently high price all the time to avoid WGCs even during peak demand. However, at times of low demand, prices are unnecessarily high, and, thus, demand is inefficiently suppressed. A second, more elaborate market design is to usesurge pricingthat responds to market conditions—the main mechanism used by Uber and Lyft. Prices are set high during peak loads, but they fall when demand is more normal. To compare both pricing policies, we calibrate our model to the data. We first analyze how a platform behaves if it can set a different price for every market (surge pricing) or if it is constrained to a single price (uniform pricing). In our baseline, we assume the platform maximizes welfare, but we obtain similar results for alternative objective functions. The optimal uniform price is at the 87th percentile of the surge price distribution: The only way to avoid the drastic loss in welfare from WGCs is to price close to the highest surge prices. Thus, surge pricing only brings modest price increases at times of high demand, whereas it allows large reductions when demand is low. We then use our calibration to quantify the welfare impact of WGCs in Manhattan. Surge pricing, as implemented by Uber in the data, averts welfare losses from WGCs that range from $3.02M to $7.25M per week (4.21%–10.13% of total welfare), with important benefits to riders, drivers, and the platform. Uber could further reduce the welfare costs of WGCs by an additional $0.69M–$1.87M per week if it priced optimally. In summary, although there is plenty of room for Uber to improve how it prices to better avoid WGCs, the current surge pricing algorithm averts the bulk of the welfare losses from WGCs. Surge pricing is a good way to avoid WGCs, but it has drawbacks. AlthoughCastillo (2023)finds, and this paper confirms, that it benefits riders, surge pricing has a widespread negative reputation among customers and regulators, who often think of it as a form of price discrimination or price gouging. Many platforms do not use surge pricing, either to avoid a backlash from consumers or because of bans. We analyze how platforms can tackle WGCs without relying on surge pricing by moving away from first dispatch. We focus on three simple protocols that avoid WGCs.3They prevent the far-away matches that lead to WGCs by doing one of three things: denying trips, making riders wait before matching them, or rematching them after their initial match. In our calibration, surge pricing outperforms all three matching protocols: In fact, it almost always Pareto dominates them in the presence of WGCs. Additionally, riders probably dislike the uncertainty that arises from denied trips, rematches, and waiting. These drawbacks might explain why Uber and Lyft use surge pricing despite its negative reputation. Platforms, however, have successfully coupled surge pricing and adjustments to the matching protocol. We analyze how they put them together to avoid WGCs. Related Work. In an early analysis of optimal pricing in radio taxi markets,Arnott (1996)briefly discusses the possibility of WGCs. He simply points out that they are Pareto dominated and thus not part of the optimal solution. Starting from his simple observation, we develop a full analysis of WGCs in ride hailing, their welfare consequences, and how to solve them. Two papers highlight issues that are closely related to WGCs.Besbes et al. (2021a)study general spatial environments. They point out that failures like WGCs arise when idle servers are depleted due to pickups. Using a model of a linear city to compare taxis and ride hailing,Feng et al. (2021)find a nonmonotonicity in waiting times that relates to the nonmonotonicity that gives rise to WGCs. Our work differs from these two papers in two ways. First, we model the dispatch system as only one part of a more complex system that also includes demand for trips and labor supply—both of which are key parts of ride-hailing markets that exacerbate WGCs. Second, we analyze different mechanisms. Besbes et al. focus on the total capacity of the system. Ride-hailing platforms cannot control capacity directly, so we focus instead on prices and matching. Feng et al. analyze one of our alternative matching protocols (denying trips), but do not consider pricing. Our work relates to a broader literature about dynamic matching, including works about vehicle routing (Bertsimas et al. 2019). A new stream of papers focuses on specific features about ride hailing.4Akbarpour et al. (2021)emphasize the high value of having some spare driver supply. Others (Özkan and Ward 2020,Kanoria 2021) solve for the optimal dynamic matching algorithm. We focus on simpler algorithms that resemble what ride-hailing platforms have implemented at a large scale. We add to this literature an analysis of WGCs, which do not occur in these works because they do not account for the fact that far-away matches deplete the system of idle drivers. Many papers analyze surge pricing in ride hailing.Cachon et al. (2017)measure the welfare effects of surge pricing, but they do not consider matching frictions like those that give rise to WGCs. Based on a structural model,Castillo (2023)also measures the welfare effects of surge pricing; his focus is not on WGCs, but his results validate our findings about WGCs.Garg and Nazerzadeh (2022)highlight the desirability of additive instead of multiplicative surge pricing, andFreund and van Ryzin (2021)show that high frequency price changes create undesirable oscillations. Our contribution to this literature is that we highlight the interaction between pricing and matching—how to price to avoid WGCs. Finally,Ahmadinejad et al. (2019)build on our work, about a single platform, to analyze the effect of WGCs on competition between platforms. Other papers focus on the spatial dimension of pricing in ride hailing, which, for simplicity, we abstract away from. Modeling ride hailing as a network of discrete locations,Bimpikis et al. (2019)find that markets perform best when prices induce balanced inflows and outflows by location.Besbes et al. (2021b)andMa et al. (2022)analyze how platforms should price accounting for drivers’ relocation choices. A more general literature models spatial equilibrium in urban transportation. Some works analyze frictions in taxi markets (Fréchette et al. 2019,Buchholz 2021), substitution between private and pooled transportation (Arora et al. 2024), the design of bike-share systems (He et al. 2021), and other aspects of ride hailing, such as riders’ value of time (Buchholz et al. 2022) and competition between platforms (Rosaia 2024).
article.2. Model: We consider a static steady-state model of ride-hailing in homogeneous space. 2.1. Trip Demand and Labor Supply Let demand beD(T,p), the number of people who request a trip given expected pickup timeTand pricep.5It is measured in trip requests per unit area and per unit time (which we refer to as “hourly”). We make the following assumption. D(T, p)is bounded and is differentiable and decreasing in both arguments. For all𝑇,𝑝≥0T,p≥0,respectively,lim𝑝→∞⁡𝐷⁡(𝑇,𝑝)=0limp→∞⁡D⁡(T,p)=0andlim𝑇→∞⁡𝐷⁡(𝑇,𝑝)=0limT→∞⁡D⁡(T,p)=0.For all𝑝≥0p≥0,the distribution of the willingness to wait has a finite mean. This states that there is a finite pool of potential riders; more riders request a trip when prices and pickup times are low; nobody is willing to pay or wait infinitely; and the distribution of willingness to wait is not too fat-tailed. Drivers decide whether to work based on expected hourly earningse. There is a supply of drivers𝐿=𝑙⁡(𝑒)L=l⁡(e), measured in drivers per unit area, which we assume is a differentiable and increasing function that is zero ate= 0 (no drivers are willing to drive for free). LetQbe the equilibrium number of hourly trips (per unit area) andτbe the commission charged by the platform. Earnings are given by𝑒=(1−𝜏)⁢𝑝⁢𝑄𝐿e=(1−τ)⁢p⁢QL. 2.2. Trip Supply Demand gives the hourly trips requested, whereas labor supply is the number of drivers working. To put both quantities together, we need a trip supply function that gives the hourly trips thatLdrivers can supply, that is, the service rate. This function arises from technological constraints that stem from the matching process. We assume that the platform uses a first-dispatch protocol: when a rider requests a trip, he is matched immediately to the closest idle driver. We consider other matching protocols inSection 6. Working drivers are in one of three states: idle (waiting to be matched), en route (picking up a rider), or driving a rider to her destination. The total number of drivers is thus equal to the sum of drivers in each one of these states. LetIbe the number of idle drivers per unit area. In steady state,tQdrivers are driving a rider, wheretis the average trip duration. This is the average time it takes to complete a trip times the number of hourly trips. Similarly,𝑇⁢𝑄T⁢Qdrivers are on their way to pick up a rider becauseTis the average time it takes a driver to pick up a rider. This last term captures a defining feature of ride-hailing markets: Pickup times affect both riders and drivers. Whenever a rider waits a long time to be picked up, the driver spends that same amount of time en route. Based on these expressions, the following identity accounts for the total density of drivers in steady state: The average pickup timeT(I), which captures the matching technology, is a decreasing function of the number of idle drivers: If there are more idle drivers, a new arriving rider will on average be matched to a driver that is closer to her, and therefore, she will have to wait less time before being picked up. We assume thatTis continuously differentiable and convexly decreases from∞∞when there are no idle drivers to zero if there are infinite idle drivers. Convexity means diminishing marginal returns of idle drivers. All these conditions are satisfied by the empirically motivated functional forms we use in our calibration and by simple functional forms that can be theoretically motivated.6 BecauseT(I) is a decreasing function, we can define its inverse,I(T), which will turn out to be more convenient for our model.I(T) can be interpreted as the number of idle drivers needed to ensure an average pickup timeT. It is easily seen thatI(T) has the same shape properties asT(I). IsolatingQfrom1and substitutingI(T) gives the desired expression for trip supply: This equation can be understood as an application of Little’s law. The hourly trips supplied is the number of busy drivers divided by the average busy time spent per trip.
article.3. Wild Goose Chases: 3.1. Good and WGC Matching Equilibria We consider equilibriaholding the price fixedbecause price is a parameter that can be chosen by the platform. Instead of clearing through prices, the market clears through pickup times.D(T,p) can be thought of as a decreasing demand function, whereTplays the role of prices andpis an exogenous demand shifter (Figure 2). Trip supplyS(T,L) is a continuous function ofT; the number of drivers workingLis a supply shifter. The behavior of the trip supply curve is summarized in the following proposition. Fix the number of working drivers L. No pickup time below T(L)is achievable. Trip supply is zero at T(L), then increases in pickup time until it reaches a maximum and then decreases until zero asymptotically. Figure 2illustrates a trip supply function that satisfies these properties. The most remarkable feature is its nonmonotonicity, which drives our main results. The intuition for this unusual characteristic is as follows. The numerator of𝑆⁡(𝑇,𝐿)=𝐿−𝐼⁡(𝑇)𝑡+𝑇S⁡(T,L)=L−I⁡(T)t+Tis increasing: if the platform wants to achieve a lower pickup time, it needs more idle drivers, which decreases the number of busy drivers and reduces the number of trips that can be supplied. This effect is the main driving force in the blue region. As pickup times increase and supply approaches its maximum, a second effect kicks in. The denominator grows as drivers spend a significant portion of their time picking up far-away passengers. Therefore, despite the fact that higher pickup times require fewer idle drivers, the total time it takes to complete a trip becomes longer, and the trip supply eventually decreases. This second effect dominates in the red region: the ride-hailing system, by trying to serve beyond its capacity, ends up sending drivers to distant locations that ultimately reduce the number of rides it can provide. This resembles hypercongestion in transportation (Walters 1961): When too many cars enter a road, speed decreases to an extent that the throughput of the road falls. Nonmonotonicity means that there are two ways to supply the same number of trips. We call the one below the maximum of supply, plotted in blue, agood outcome. The one above the maximum, plotted in red, has a longer pickup time. The latter situation is evidently inefficient: it achieves the same number of trips with the same number of drivers, but with higher pickup times. We call these situations WGCs. The market is in a WGC if it is in the decreasing region of the trip supply function. If the platform has to choose an equilibrium at some point along the good region of the trip supply curve, it faces a tradeoff between the number of trips and the pickup time. But if it is in a WGC, it no longer faces a tradeoff: Moving upward along the curve both decreases the number of trips and increases pickup times. Thus, it becomes evident that the platform would prefer to move down the supply curve to return to a good outcome. Notably, WGCs do not happen with street-hail matching, wherein riders hail taxis on the streets.7Idle drivers roam the streets until they find a rider, but once they find one the trip starts immediately, so the pickup time is zero. The driver accounting identity is then𝐿=𝐼+𝑡⁢𝑄L=I+t⁢Q. There is a decreasing functioñ𝐼⁡(𝑇)I˜⁡(T)that tells how many idle drivers are required to provide a waiting time ofT(how long the rider waits on the street before an idle taxi drives by): The more idle drivers who are roaming the streets, the less time a rider should expect to wait. IsolatingQleads to an increasing supply functioñ𝑆⁡(𝑇,𝐿)=𝐿−̃𝐼⁡(𝑇)𝑡S˜⁡(T,L)=L−I˜⁡(T)t, which we also show inFigure 2. The intuition is the same as in good outcomes for ride-hailing markets: Decreasing waiting times requires more idle taxis, which reduces the number of drivers available to take passengers to their destination. However, the opposing effect is no longer present, and so trip supply is no longer nonmonotonic. 3.1.1. Diagnosing WGCs. WGCs can be easily diagnosed in the data. Letslackbe𝑠=𝐼𝑇⁢𝑄s=IT⁢Q, the ratio of idle drivers to en route drivers, an adimensional measure of the availability of drivers. Also let𝜖𝑇𝐼ϵITbe the elasticity of pickup time with respect to the density of idle drivers (this elasticity is negative). WGCs take place when the derivative of trip supply is negative, which, after some algebra, can be rewritten as To understand this inequality, note from (1) that the number of drivers taking passengers to their destination is𝑡⁢𝑄=𝐿−𝐼−𝑇⁢𝑄t⁢Q=L−I−T⁢Q. A 1% increase in the pickup time requires0.01⁢𝑄⁢𝑇0.01⁢Q⁢Tmore en route drivers, and hence,0.01⁢𝑄⁢𝑇0.01⁢Q⁢Tfewer drivers driving passengers. However, the increase in pickup times also decreases the number of idle drivers by1|𝜖𝑇𝐼|1|ϵIT|percent, which means0.01⁢𝐼⁢1|𝜖𝑇𝐼|0.01⁢I⁢1|ϵIT|more drivers are driving passengers. The net effect on the number of drivers driving passengers—and, thus, on the number of trips—is negative whenever𝑄⁢𝑇>𝐼⁢1|𝜖𝑇𝐼|Q⁢T>I⁢1|ϵIT|, which is equivalent to (3). WGCs can thus be diagnosed by focusing on two elements:𝜖𝑇𝐼ϵIT, which measures the relative magnitudes of changes in pickup times and idle drivers along the curveT(I), and slack(𝐼𝑇⁢𝑄)(IT⁢Q), which measures the relative impact those changes have on the number of trips that the system can provide. Expression (3) makes the theory straightforward to test. The number of idle drivers and the number of en route drivers are directly observable in the data, so slack can be easily computed. Although𝜖𝑇𝐼ϵITis not directly observable,T(I) can easily be fit to the data. InSection 5.1, we obtain estimates of𝜖𝑇𝐼ϵITbetween −0.25 and −0.45. 3.2. Equilibrium We now put together the three parts our model: demand, labor supply, and trip supply. The first condition for equilibrium is that trip supply and demand must be equal: InFigure 2, this means that supply and demand intersect in equilibrium. As illustrated in the figure, the intersection can be a good outcome or a WGC. The second equilibrium condition is that the number of drivers working has to be equal to labor supply: An equilibrium is a joint solution in (T,L,Q) ofEquations (4)and (5). The solution toEquation (4)givesQas a function ofL. The solution toEquation (5)givesLas a function ofQ. Thus, they can be thought of as loci in the (Q,L) plane with equilibria at their intersections. An equilibrium is stable if, after a small perturbation, iteratively applying these two functions converges to the equilibrium. A formal definition, which requires further notation, is stated in Online Appendix A.2. The following proposition guarantees the existence of stable equilibria. A stable equilibrium always exists, possibly with𝑄=𝐿=0Q=L=0.If there are multiple equilibria, then it is equivalent to order them by Q or L. The highest equilibrium is always stable. It is not difficult to construct reasonable functional forms that lead to multiple equilibria, but none of our calibrations have more than one equilibrium. Throughout our theoretical analysis, we select the highest and thus the best equilibrium. 3.3. Revenue, Surplus, and Welfare At an equilibrium with pricepandQtrips, the platform gets revenue𝜏⁢𝑝⁢𝑄τ⁢p⁢Q. We need additional assumptions to define rider surplus, driver surplus, and welfare. First, let the social cost of driversC(L) be the integral of the inverse supply curve, so that𝐶′(𝐿)=𝑙−1⁡(𝐿)C′(L)=l−1⁡(L)for allL. This is a standard cost function that is increasing and convex. Driver surplus is equal to earnings minus cost: To define consumer surplus, letU(Q,T) be gross utility. It satisfies the following standard conditions8: Gross utility U(Q, T)is continuously differentiable in(Q, T). It is decreasing in T and increasing in Q with𝑈𝑄⁡(𝑄,𝑇)=𝑝UQ⁡(Q,T)=p. We define rider surplus as Finally, total welfare is gross utility minus social cost—or, equivalently, the sum of rider surplus, driver surplus, and revenue: 3.4. Pricing and WGCs We now analyze how pricing affects the equilibrium in this market. In our main analysis, we hold the platform’s commissionτfixed, as was historically the case. At the end of this section, we discuss certain policies that varyτ. In Online Appendix B, we characterize optimal pricing when the platform can also adjust its commission. We first look at the local effect of pricing under WGCs. Starting from a WGC equilibrium, a price decrease increases pickup times and decreases the number of trips, the number of drivers working, driver surplus, revenue, and total welfare. The effect on rider surplus is ambiguous. This result highlights that it is good to increase prices when the market is in a WGCs. A price increase is not always a Pareto improvement—rider surplus might decrease—but it is in our calibrations. This proposition might not be too surprising. If prices start from a low base, one might expect a further decrease to have the effects described in this proposition. The key feature of WGCs is that they can be diagnosed, and because of a feedback cycle, they lead to sharper effects than in other markets, as we now argue. Consider a WGC equilibrium like point A inFigure 3(a)with labor supplyL0and suppose there is a small price decrease. Consider first the effect before labor supply adjusts. Demand shifts outwards until the equilibrium is at point B, in which case there are fewer trips than at point A. Earnings then decrease through two channels: lower prices and fewer trips. Now consider the first-order labor response to such decrease in earnings: a lower labor supplyL1. This moves the equilibrium to point C, which is far to the left of point B. This occurs because both supply and demand are decreasing, causing a feedback cycle in which a lower number of trips and higher pickup times reinforce each other. A second feedback cycle further reinforces these effects. The decrease in trips from B to C further reduces earnings, so labor supply further decreases to a new levelL2(not shown in the figure). The number of trips declines further, which reduces earnings and further decreases labor supply. The cycle operates until the market equilibrium settles down at point D, with labor supply𝐿∞L∞. This iteration process converges because the equilibrium is stable (Proposition 2). All these steps mean that the market eventually ends up with substantially fewer trips and substantially higher pickup times than at point A, despite the fact that the original change in prices was small. We formalize this analysis in Online Appendix A.3, where we find expressions for derivatives of the number of trips, labor supply, and pickup times that highlight these feedback cycles. After the price decrease fromFigure 3(a), driver surplus decreases because more drivers working means a movement down the labor supply curve. Rider surplus goes down because of the higher price, but it goes up because of the lower pickup time. Which effect is larger depends on the functional form for utility, so we cannot make any further statements without stronger assumptions. Although the effect on rider surplus is ambiguous, total welfare unambiguously decreases. Gross utility decreases both through fewer trips and higher pickup times. The cost of drivers decreases by(1−𝜏)⁢𝑝⁢𝜖𝑙1+𝜖𝑙(1−τ)⁢p⁢ϵl1+ϵl, whereϵlis the elasticity of supply, but this is more than offset by the decrease in gross utility ofpthrough fewer trips. We prove all these results in Online Appendix A.3. The following proposition shows that prices can be used to avoid WGCs. Suppose that the highest equilibrium of the market at price p is in a WGC. Then there exists some higher price𝑝′>𝑝p′>pat which the highest equilibrium is no longer in a WGC. This result arises from the assumption that demand is bounded and goes to zero as price goes to infinity. As the price increases, the demand curve shifts downward, and its maximum eventually becomes less than maximum supply. In this case there is at least one good equilibrium.
article.4. Empirical Evidence of WGCs: 4.1. Data We use proprietary Uber data from Manhattan between December 1st, 2016, and May 14, 2017.9We focus on UberX (Uber’s main product, which matches riders to independent drivers) and UberPool (which matches multiple riders to the same driver) because the set of drivers working for both products is the same.10We aggregate our data into half-hour periods. Spatially, we aggregate it into 10regions, the Public Use Microdata Areas (PUMAs) in Manhattan (Online Appendix D presents a map). This intermediate level of aggregation matches best the steady state in homogeneous space that we analyze in our model. Our analysis thus abstracts away from high-resolution spatial heterogeneity and transient dynamics, which are only likely to exacerbate WGCs as they create larger imbalances between supply and demand. On the supply side, we observe the total number of driver-minutes spent in each one of the three driver states (idle, en route, and driving a rider). On the demand side, we see how many riders open the app and choose UberX. We also see all trips requested and whether they were completed. For trips that were not completed, we observe why: either the rider cancelled, the driver canceled, or the rider could not be matched to a driver. Finally, we observe data on surge pricing. A surge multiplier, which multiplies the base fare to obtain the final fare, is set every two minutes in small hexagon-shaped locations that are either 600 (before January 15) or 350 meters (after January 15) across. The surge multiplier is a function of (a) the number of riders that have opened the app nearby during the last few minutes and (b) the number of drivers that have been available nearby during the last few minutes. We average surge multipliers by half hour and region to match the structure of our data. All this aggregation should dull the observability of our key predictions; thus, the relative sharpness of our findings, which is in line with our theory, is all the more striking. The matching process by which riders and drivers are matched resembles very closely the idealized first-dispatch protocol from our model. When a rider requests a trip, it is offered to the nearest available driver, who has a few seconds to accept. If the driver does not accept, the trip is offered to the second nearest available driver, and so on. If drivers keep rejecting trips, the process stops when there are no more available drivers within 2.5 km of the driver, in which case the rider is shown a message saying there are no drivers in the area. A large fraction of trip offers are accepted (more than 80%): Drivers do not know the trip destination before the rider gets in the car, which creates few incentives for cherry picking. Trips are occasionally offered to drivers who are about to drop off a rider nearby, but this form of matching accounts for fewer than 15% of trips. 4.2. Trip Supply We now show an empirical analogue ofFigure 2, which exhibits the nonmonotonic trip supply curve that leads to WGCs. This provides evidence that WGCs occur in the data. We first illustrate this using raw data, and then use a more nuanced regression methodology. 4.2.1. Raw Data. Our model predicts that trip supplyS(T,L) is nonmonotonic given a fixed number of drivers. The data shows a lot of variation in the number of drivers, so we first split the sample into quintiles of the number of drivers by region.Figure 4(a)shows how the number of trips behaves as a function of pickup times for each quintile. Every observation represents averages for a one half-hour period in one region. The horizontal axis represents the number of trips completed; because the scale of this variable varies widely across regions, we divide it by the average number of trips completed in each region. The vertical axis is the average ETA—the pickup time shown to riders when they request a trip. All quintiles show the backward bending relation we expect from our theory. Online Appendix E.2 shows that, for this figure and for the rest of this section, we obtain very similar results if we use the realized pickup time instead of the ETA. We prefer to use the ETA to avoid selection bias from high cancellation rates when riders see a high pickup time. Additional factors affect trip supply beyond those included in our theoretical model—in particular, traffic speed, weather, and the average trip length. To account for them, we plot inFigure 4(b)the residuals of regressions of the ETA and the log number of trips on region dummies, the number of drivers, average traffic speed, average trip length, temperature, precipitation, and the square of each one of these variables. The nonmonotonic relation persists after controlling for confounders. The curve bends backward when the ETA residual is above 1.05, which accounts for 10.9% of observations. This suggests there are times when the platform fails to set prices high enough to avoid the worst market outcomes. The decreasing part of the curve roughly corresponds to slack below 0.35. This is consistent with estimates that we will present inSection 4.3, according to which WGCs should take place below a threshold that is between 0.25 and 0.45. 4.2.2. Regression Analysis. To interpret the curves inFigure 4as the trip supply, the main source of variation tracing them out must be demand shifts—and not shifts of the trip supply curve. This concern is partially addressed by controlling for the most important trip supply shifters (labor supply, traffic conditions, weather, and the average trip length), but probably there are additional shifters that we do not observe. To address this concern, we now perform a regression analysis that uses demand shifts as instrumental variables. We run regressions of the following form: The main challenge when estimating Regression (9) is simultaneity: If there is a positive shock to trip supply, pickup times mechanically go down as the market equilibrium moves down the demand curve inFigure 2. To tackle this issue, we instrument the ETA with the number of riders who open the app, regardless of whether they end up requesting a trip.11The key to this identification strategy is the fact that riders only observe prices and ETAsafterthey have opened the app; our instrument therefore cannot be affected by supply shocks riders did not expect before interacting with the app. Given that we include region by hour-of-the-week fixed effects𝛼𝑟,ℎ⁡(𝑡)αr,h⁡(t)and controls𝛾⁢𝑋𝑟⁢𝑡γ⁢Xr⁢t, simultaneity issues would only persist if there exist shocks to the supply curve that (a) operate within rather than across region by hour-of-the-week cells, (b) riders know of before opening the app, and (c) are unrelated to the variables we control for—labor supply, traffic speed, weather, and the average trip length. Riders are likely to respond to many types of trip supply shocks. However, we are not particularly worried that those shocks could bias our estimates because every example we can think of violates at least one of (a), (b), or (c). Any unexpected shock violates (b). Riders may expect higher pickup times at some hours, such as rush hour, but that constitutes variation across hours of the week, so it violates (a). They may also expect higher pickup times when it rains or when roads are closed, but we control for weather and traffic speed, so these shocks violate (c). They may expect higher pickup times when few drivers are working, but these shocks also violate (c) because we control for labor supply. Finally, riders may expect high pickup times at the end of concerts or similar events, but these events are demand shocks—exactly the kind of variation we want to use to trace the trip supply curve—so it is not a threat to our identification strategy. Figure 5(a)presents estimates ofEquation (9)by least squares and by instrumental variables. Both estimated curves are nonmonotonic, as predicted by our model.Figure 5(b)presents scatterplots that show the raw patterns in the data driving these two regressions.12The fact that the right panel exhibits a nonmonotonicity means that there exists a range in which a higher number of people opening the app—which drives the variation in the predicted ETA—is associated with a smaller number of trips. This seemingly contradictory pattern is evidence of WGCs and their unusual properties: an increase in demand results in fewer trips taking place. 4.3. WGCs in the Data We now use the criterion𝑠<−𝜖𝑇𝐼s<−ϵITto determine when WGCs occur in the data. 4.3.1. Modeling Pickup Times. To compute the elasticity of pickup times, we split our sample into 480 groups, each one of which corresponds to a PUMA during one hour of the day either on weekdays or weekends. Group by group, we use trip-level data to estimate a nonparametric fit of the pickup time as a function of the straight-line distance between the pickup location and the location where the driver was at the time of matching (see Online Appendix E.1). We then computeT(I) by integrating the predicted pickup time over the distribution of the distance to the nearest driver, given the density of idle driversI.13For every observation, we compute the elasticity ofT(I) at the observed number of driversI.Figure 6(a)plots the distribution of pickup time elasticities that we estimate. Most values are between −0.45 and −0.25, and the mean is −0.364. 4.3.2. Prevalence of WGCs. We find that the market is in a WGC—that is, slack is below the pickup time elasticity—in 4.34% of observations (half-hour periods in one region), which account for 6.41% of the trips in our data.Figure 6, (b) and (c), breaks down the prevalence of WGCs by hour of the week and by region. On weekdays, WGCs are most frequent during the early morning and the morning rush hour, and they are also common during the afternoon rush hour. They are infrequent during weekends, except for a mild peak at night and a spike during bar hours. Spatially, WGCs are most common in the East Side—especially in the Lower East Side—which consists of neighborhoods with a high density of residents that request morning rush hour trips. 4.3.3. Performance Measures. We now show evidence that market performance deteriorates drastically when WGCs occur.Figure 7(a)plots the fraction of requested trips that are subsequently cancelled by riders. It plots them against𝑠+𝜖𝑇𝐼s+ϵIT, which measures how far slack is from the threshold at which WGCs start to occur. This is a more nuanced version ofFigure 1, which only has slacksin the horizontal axis. The cancellation rate increases as we move to the left, which is not surprising: lower slack means low driver availability, which harms rider experience and should cause more cancellations. What is remarkable is how a mild relation suddenly spikes as slack approaches the threshold for WGCs.Figure 7(b)shows similar results for the average ETA. It also shows a sudden spike as slack approaches the threshold for WGCs. The rapid deterioration relates to our analysis fromSection 3.3, which shows how sensitive the market is to small price deviations from WGC equilibria. As we show inSection 5.1, this too is consistent with our calibration. These figures show that the surge multiplier rarely goes above 1 when slack is above 1. Conversely, the surge multiplier becomes larger as slack goes down, and we see especially high multipliers for slack below 0.5. This is exactly what one would expect if Uber reacts to slack (or to closely related measures) by increasing multipliers. Despite this behavior, WGCs do take place, as evidenced by observations with low slack and bad performance measures. This indicates that Uber might have benefitted from increasing prices even more aggressively at troublesome times, as we corroborate inSection 5.2.
article.5. Surge Pricing: In the rest of the paper, we analyze the different mechanisms platforms can use to avoid WGCs. We first focus on pricing, the main mechanism used by Uber and Lyft. Proposition 4shows that all that is needed to take the market out of a WGC is to increase prices. We analyze how platforms can use that insight to set up a market-wide pricing policy, both with and without surge pricing. We calibrate our model and apply it to quantitatively analyze the impacts of different pricing policies. 5.1. Model Calibration 5.1.1. Functional Form Assumptions. Letλbe the arrival rate of potential riders. Letr(p) be the fraction of those riders that is willing to pay a surge multiplierp(which we also refer to as price).14Letg(T) be the fraction that is willing to wait a timeT. We assume that willingness to pay and wait are independent, so demand is𝐷⁡(𝑇,𝑝)=𝜆⁢𝑔⁡(𝑇)⁢𝑟⁡(𝑝)D⁡(T,p)=λ⁢g⁡(T)⁢r⁡(p). This assumption would be violated if both decisions are correlated, but it is not clear whether the correlation should be positive or negative.15 For simplicity, we assume that utility does not depend directly on the pickup time, although it does depend indirectly through the number of trips requested. Taking into account the disutility of waiting would only make the effects of WGCs stronger because high pickup times hurt consumers even more. Gross utility equals the gross utility per rider who is willing to wait times the number of riders who are willing to wait:𝑈⁡(𝑝,𝑇)=𝜆⁢𝑔⁡(𝑇)⁢[∫∞𝑝𝑟⁢(𝑝′)⁢𝑑⁢𝑝′+𝑝⁢𝑟⁡(𝑝)]U⁡(p,T)=λ⁢g⁡(T)⁢[∫p∞r⁢(p′)⁢d⁢p′+p⁢r⁡(p)]. We assume that the willingness to pay of riders has a lognormal distribution with parameterσ= 1, which fits very well the shape of the income distribution in New York City.16We choose the remaining parameterμ, a rescaling of the distribution, to fit the elasticities inCohen et al. (2023). We computer(p) as the cumulative density function (CDF) of this distribution. Similarly, we assume that willingness to wait has a lognormal distribution with mode five minutes and variance such that the elasticity ofg(T)—the CDF of this distribution—agrees with the estimate from Cohen et al. We assume a constant-elasticity labor supply,𝑙⁡(𝑒)=𝐴⁢(𝑒1+1𝜖𝑙)𝜖𝑙l⁡(e)=A⁢(e1+1ϵl)ϵl. We set𝜖𝑙=1.2ϵl=1.2based onAngrist et al. (2021). Finally, pickup timesT(I) follow functional forms that we estimate directly from the data and which we discuss below. 5.1.2. Calibration to Different Markets. Our model has two free parameters17: the arrival rate of potential ridersλ(a demand shifter) and the overall supply shifterA. For every market, we back them out as the values that are consistent with the number of trips requested and the number of drivers working that we observe. 5.2. Simple Analysis We start with a simple analysis to explain the intuition behind our main results for pricing, which we present inSection 5.3. We calibrate our model to three markets: theaveragemarket, which represents average market conditions; theweakmarket, which represents off-peak conditions around noon; and thestrongmarket, which represents rush-hour conditions with driver scarcity. We let the pickup timeT(I) follow a simple functional form that matches aggregate data quite well (see Online Appendix E.1). Online Appendix E.3 presents further details about this calibration. Figure 8shows how revenue and welfare behave as a function of price. The opaque lines represent the average market. Dashed lines represent the region in which WGCs occur. There is a drastic drop in welfare to the left of the WGC threshold. This is evidence that WCG equilibria can lead to dramatic welfare losses because rider surplus, driver surplus, profits, and the number of trips all decrease drastically, as we show in Online Appendix E.4. To the right of the threshold, on the other hand, any price increase benefits drivers and the platform, but it hurts passengers. Net changes in welfare are thus not overly large: a 30% price increase from the optimum only decreases welfare by 2.5%—much less than the 37% welfare decrease that results from a 30% price decrease. To highlight that the drop in welfare is a unique feature of a ride-hailing platform that uses first dispatch, we also compute welfare and revenue if Uber used the matching technology of street-hail taxis, assuming that demand and labor supply stay the same.18The results are the semitransparent lines inFigure 8. As with ride-hailing, there is a faster decrease in welfare to the left than to the right of the maximum: A 30% price increase from the optimum decreases welfare by 3.2%, whereas a 30% price decrease lowers it by 8.9%. However, the asymmetry is much less striking than for ride-hailing. We can also see that if prices are set properly, ride-hailing achieves higher welfare, but street-hailing behaves better for low prices in the WGC region. The revenue maximizing price for ride hailing is around 2.2. However, Uber very rarely sets the surge multiplier above 2. This apparent puzzle is solved after considering what Uber actually maximizes. The demand curve is calibrated to the short-run elasticity estimated byCohen et al. (2023). A price of 2.2 thus maximizes revenuein the short run. However, Uber’s objective function is long-run shareholder value, which is likely to be closely related to consumer satisfaction (i.e., rider surplus). This is consistent with two papers that find that prices are inconsistent with short-run profit maximization:Castillo (2023)finds that prices are consistent with consumer surplus maximization, whereasRosaia (2024)finds that they are consistent with maximizing welfare plus 0.52 times profits. 5.2.1. Surge and Uniform Pricing. We now analyze how the platform can use pricing to avoid WGCs. Suppose that it only faces the weak and strong markets. We assume that its short-run goal is to maximize total welfare. Our findings also hold if the objective function is any other concave function that falls rapidly to the left of the optimum as the market enters a WGC, such as the objective functions estimated byCastillo (2023)andRosaia (2024)(see Online Appendix E.4). Figure 9shows welfare for the strong and weak markets. As the price goes down, the market enters the WGC region and both markets exhibit the sharp drop in welfare we observed in the average market. The drop happens at a higher price in the strong market: because demand is higher, prices do not need to go far down to enter a WGC. Consistent withProposition 4, welfare is increasing in the WGC region, and high-enough prices take the market out of a WGC. We consider two different ways Uber can choose prices. Withuniform pricing, Uber chooses the single price that maximizes total welfare (the vertical gray line). Withsurge pricing, Uber chooses the welfare-maximizing price for each market (the vertical dotted lines). The uniform price is very close to the surge price for the strong market. Whereas the surge price for the strong market is only 3% above the uniform price, the surge price for the weak market is 39% below. The reason for this extreme asymmetry is the sudden drop in welfare as prices go down and the market enters a WGC. With uniform pricing, the platform has little freedom to set prices below the strong market optimum because it would enter a WGC with a substantial welfare drop. This means that, relative to uniform pricing, surge pricing leads to a significant reduction of prices in weak markets, whereas it only leads to modest price increases in strong markets. This differs from the perception that surge pricing hurts consumers due to high prices. If anything, drivers should be the ones worried because surge pricing might result in low prices during times of low demand. All these findings are consistent with the results ofCastillo (2023), which are based on a more detailed estimation of a structural model. Uber never allows the surge multiplier to go below one.Figure 9suggests an explanation for this choice. Although the welfare-maximizing price for the weak market is 0.65, there is only a minor welfare loss (3.5%) if the price is set at one because welfare is relatively flat to the right of the optimum. To avoid facing criticism for not paying drivers well and for exercising predatory pricing, Uber might avoid pricing below one at the expense of a small percent of welfare. 5.3. Main Counterfactual Analysis We now undertake a more realistic analysis, in which the platform faces one market for each half-hour period in one region. We calibrateλandAto each market, and we let the pickup timeT(I) be the function that we estimate inSection 4.3for the corresponding hour-of-the-week and region. Online Appendix E.5 explains our calibration procedure in detail, and it shows figures of the main observables we use for calibration as well as the values of the main parameters that we back out. 5.3.1. Surge and Uniform Pricing. With surge pricing, the platform chooses prices market by market.Figure 10shows the distribution of the welfare maximizing prices across hours of the week. The pattern for ride hailing is similar Monday through Thursday, with the highest prices in the early morning and during the morning and afternoon rush hours. Prices are highest during the afternoon and evening on Fridays, which are high demand times, and weekends have high prices because of low supply. In Online Appendix E.5, we show similar results under the assumption that Uber maximizes the objective functions estimated byCastillo (2023)andRosaia (2024). We also show that the optimal prices roughly follow the observed prices, and that prices are higher when WGCs are more prevalent. Last, we also analyze spatial patterns; we find little variation across regions. The optimal uniform price, 1.401, is at the 87th percentile of the distribution of surge prices, consistent with our finding that a high uniform price is necessary to avoid WGCs. A similar calculation for street-hail taxis shows that the optimal uniform price, 1.528, is only at the 69th percentile. The uniform price is similar with both matching technologies, but surge prices are substantially higher with taxis (averaging 1.370) than for ride-hailing (averaging 1.076). 5.3.2. Welfare Losses from WGCs. We now use our calibrated model to quantify the welfare losses due to WGCs. We simulate several counterfactuals in which Uber uses different pricing policies. In every scenario, we compute the equilibrium for every market, defined as a one half-hour period in every region.Table 1presents the changes in welfare in every scenario relative to the status quo. Table 1.Welfare Changes in Counterfactual Scenarios Table 1.Welfare Changes in Counterfactual Scenarios Notes.This table shows welfare for counterfactual scenarios. All numbers are changes relative to the status quo. Columns (2) and (3) present changes in total welfare. Columns (4) and (5) present changes in the welfare of markets that are in a WGC in the status quo for rows 1–4 and in the uniform pricing scenario for rows 5 and 6. Columns (6)–(11) decompose total welfare changes into its components. The first four rows present counterfactuals with more sophisticated pricing than in the status quo. In “Price no WGC,” we increase prices in WGC markets all the way to the point where each market ceases to be in a WGC. In “Price no WGC +0.05,” we raise surge multipliers by an additional 0.05. In “Optimum multiplier” and “Optimum multiplier ≥ 1,” we set the welfare maximizing multipliers (subject top≥ 1 for the latter case). In the last two rows, we set uniform multipliers at the status quo average and at the level that maximizes welfare. 5.3.2.1. Welfare Losses of WGCs in the Data. Our first goal is to quantify the extent of welfare losses that result from WGCs in the data. In the first row ofTable 1, we consider a scenario in which Uber increases the price in every WGC market precisely enough to take the market out of a WGC. Total welfare increases by $0.16M per week (0.21% of total welfare, or 2.9% of welfare in WGC markets). All market participants benefit, as rider surplus, driver surplus, and revenue all increase.19 This scenario measures welfare losses due to WGCs in the strictest sense, as prices only increase until WGCs cease. However, one should not stop at that point: Being at the threshold of the WGC region is harmful for the same reason that WGCs themselves are harmful, so one should further increase prices to take the market far from the WGC region. With this in mind, we explore two additional scenarios. First, we measure welfare changes when surge multipliers are 0.05 higher than necessary to end WGCs. This provides a tighter lower bound for the welfare harm attributable to WGCs. As shown in the second row ofTable 1, total welfare is higher by $0.69M per week (0.96% of total welfare), and the welfare in WGC markets is 7.0% higher. Second, we set the surge multipliers that maximize welfare (as inFigure 10), subject to the constraint that prices cannot be lower than one. Row 4 ofTable 1shows that welfare increases by $1.87M per week (2.60% of total welfare). This is an upper bound for the welfare losses from WGCs: welfare increases with optimal price because WGCs are eliminated, but also more generally because demand-supply imbalances are resolved. In summary, our analysis shows that the social costs of WGCs in the data are between $0.69M and $1.87M per week (between 0.96% and 2.60% of total welfare), a sizable number when considering that less than 10% of markets are in WGCs. 5.3.2.2. Welfare Losses Averted by Surge Pricing. We now quantify the extent to which Uber, through surge pricing, already mitigates potential welfare losses from WGCs. To that end, we compare the status quo relative to two uniform pricing counterfactuals. In the first one, the multiplier is 1.076, the average multiplier in the data. This represents the most naive form of pricing, in which the platform does nothing to avoid WGCs—not even increase overall prices. In the second scenario, Uber sets the welfare maximizing uniform multiplier, 1.401. This is a more sophisticated form of pricing that is able to mitigate WGCs to some extent by optimally setting the overall level of prices. A comparison with these two counterfactuals gives us upper and lower bounds for the welfare losses mitigated by surge pricing in the data. Relative to a uniform price at 1.076 (row 5 ofTable 1), the way Uber prices in the data achieves a welfare gain of $7.27M per week, which amounts to 10.13% of welfare. These gains do not solely arise from eliminating WGCs. However, when focusing on markets that are in a WGC with a uniform multiplier, we observe welfare gains of $7.25M per week. This means that surge pricing, as implemented by Uber, has a negligible effect on markets that are not in a WGC. We can thus conclude that the welfare benefits of surge pricing, as implemented by Uber, primarily come from avoiding WGCs. If we compare the status quo relative to a uniform multiplier of 1.401 (row 6 ofTable 1), we find that surge pricing averts losses of $3.02M per week. In summary, our findings indicate that, by using surge pricing, Uber successfully mitigates welfare losses from WGCs of $3.02M–$7.25M per week. Because we previously showed that Uber can at most reduce welfare losses by an additional $1.87M per week, we can conclude that the current surge pricing algorithm averts the majority of potential welfare losses arising from WGCs. 5.3.3. Downsides of Surge Pricing. Our results show that surge pricing is a good mechanism for avoiding WGCs. However, it has some drawbacks that have limited its adoption in certain markets. Perhaps most importantly, there is a widespread perception that it is a form of price discrimination—or even price gouging—that hurts riders. That perception can be attributed to the belief that surge pricing mostly leads to increases in prices. In contrast, we find that surge pricing mostly leads to lower prices and higher rider surplus, consistent with the empirical analysis inCastillo (2023). Even though surge pricing is good for riders, the mere fact that people believe otherwise is undesirable for platforms, particularly in a market with network externalities in which every additional customer may bring in many more riders in the future. For that reason, some ride hailing companies like DiDi, the largest ride-hailing company in China, have chosen to avoid surge pricing. People have also criticized surge pricing on the grounds that it might lead to low driver earnings at times of low demand (Goncharova 2017). Perhaps for this reason Uber never sets the multiplier below one.
article.6. Matching Protocols That Avoid WGCs: We now analyze alternative matching protocols and whether they can avoid WGCs. 6.1. Three Matching Protocols We first focus on three matching protocols that can avoid WGCs. The protocols are intentionally simple: they depend on a single parameter, just like surge pricing. Our goal is not to find optimal matching protocols, which is a complicated dynamic optimization problem outside the scope of this paper. Instead, we focus on three simple protocols that are specifically designed to avoid the far-away pickups that give rise to WGCs: Deny: After a rider requests a trip, she is matched to the nearest available driver if he is within a distanceR. If the nearest driver is beyond that distance, the rider is denied a trip. Wait: After a rider requests a trip, she is matched to the nearest available driver if he is within a distanceR. If the nearest driver is farther away, the rider is made to wait until some driver drops off a passenger and becomes available within a distanceRfrom the rider. Rematch: Riders are immediately matched to the nearest available driver. If at any later time the matched driver is at a distance greater thanRfrom the rider and another driver drops off a passenger within a distanceRfrom the rider, the rider is rematched to the closer driver. Deny and Wait assume that all riders comply: If they are denied a trip, they leave the market, and if they are told to wait, they wait until being matched. In reality, if riders are denied a trip they can relogin until they get a trip (in practice equivalent to waiting), and if they are told to wait they can choose to leave (in practice equivalent to being denied). Thus, if some riders do not comply, the market ends up in a hybrid between Deny and Wait. Deny and Wait should thus be thought of as two extremes of what can happen if trips are denied or if riders are told to wait. All three protocols depend on a radiusR, which we call themaximum dispatch radius(MDR). Its purpose is to ensure that no matches make drivers go too far to pick up a rider. AsRincreases, a larger fraction of riders are matched immediately to the driver who picks them up, and all protocols approach first dispatch as𝑅→∞R→∞. Online Appendix C explains in detail how we model these three matching protocols. We now show that they can be used to avoid WGCs. We focus on the strong market fromSection 5.2, and we set a surge multiplier of 0.9 so that the market is in a WGC with first dispatch.Figure 11shows how welfare behaves as the MDR changes. We measure welfare as a percent of the highest welfare that can be achieved with surge pricing—the maximum inFigure 8. WhenRis high (i.e., first dispatch), welfare is 78% of the maximum, despite that the price is not far from the optimum (1.03) because the market is in a WGC. As the MDR decreases, welfare increases with all three protocols, and eventually the market goes out of a WGC. With Deny and Wait, as the MDR further decreases, welfare eventually reaches a maximum and then starts to decrease: Too many trips are denied or too many riders are made to wait. With Rematch, there is a point where the market discontinuously switches to a regime in which every rider is matched to a driver far away, only to be rematched to another driver who drops off a passenger nearby.20None of the alternative protocols achieves more than 94% of the maximum welfare with surge pricing. 6.1.1. Drawbacks of the Three Protocols. We now discuss some drawbacks of these protocols. We illustrate our discussion with some numerical results about the strong market with a price of 0.9, as inFigure 11. Online Appendix C.7 presents a detailed graphical quantification. With Deny, a substantial fraction of riders are denied a trip, which is especially harmful since many high-willingness-to-pay riders do not obtain a trip. To take the strong market out of a WGC, for instance, 6% of trips must be denied. At the welfare maximizing MDR, around 10% of trips are denied. Although denying trips is desirable in terms of welfare, systematically denying trips goes directly against one of the main features that, according to ride-hailing companies, differentiates them from taxi markets: reliability. Riders who are denied a trip are also likely to request a trip from a competitor and might decide not to return to the platform in the future. For these reasons, platforms might be unwilling to rely on this mechanism to control WGCs. With Wait, taking the strong market out of a WGC requires an average waiting time of only 10 seconds. This average waiting time goes up to 35 seconds at the welfare maximizing MDR of𝑅=0.4kmR=0.4km. Such brief waiting times would not be problematic if all riders waited to be matched. However, riders that are asked to wait an uncertain amount of time are likely to open a competing app to check if the pickup time is lower, even if, in the end, they only need to wait a few seconds. The uncertainty that comes with Wait thus encourages riders to shop around, increasing the chances of them switching to competing platforms. With Rematch, the fraction of rematches is small for a high MDR. However, after the regime change that takes place around a MDR of 1 km for the strong market, the fraction of rematches suddenly shoots up to one. At that point tentative matching simply becomes a trick to make riders wait for a better match without explicitly telling them that their current driver will not pick them up. Customers might end up losing confidence in the platform, an outcome that platforms may prefer to avoid. Rematching is also undesirable because customers expect a reliable estimate of their pickup time, which is not possible if the rider will be subsequently rematched. 6.1.2. Efficiency of Alternative Matching Protocols. We now quantify the extent to which these three protocols are able to mitigate the welfare losses from WGCs. As inSection 5.3.2, we compute equilibria market by market, defined as a one half-hour period in every region.Table 2measures welfare for different mechanisms that can be used to avoid WGCs. Everything is measured relative to the first row, which presents results for optimal surge pricing with first dispatch, subject to the constraint that𝑝≥1p≥1(row 4 inTable 1). Table 2.Welfare Under Alternative Mechanisms Table 2.Welfare Under Alternative Mechanisms Notes.This table presents welfare with different mechanisms. The left panel shows the total welfare for all markets, whereas the right panel presents welfare in markets that are in a WGC in the data. Because of computational constraints, we focus on a random sample of 2,000 markets in each panel. All numbers are percentages of the quantity with the welfare maximizing surge multiplier subject to𝑝≥1p≥1(“optimal pricing”). “No mechanism” represents first dispatch with a uniform multiplier at 1.076.” Status quo” refers to first dispatch with the multipliers observed in the data. Rows 4–6 represent the three alternative protocols (Deny, Wait, and Rematch) with a uniform multiplier at the average from the data, where the MDRRis the one that maximizes total welfare, market by market. The last three rows represent the three alternative protocols, whereRis fixed at 750 m and where the surge multiplier is the one we observe in the data. In rows 4–7 we present welfare with Deny, Wait, and Rematch. We set the surge multiplier at 1.076—the average multiplier in the data—and we find the MDRRthat maximizes welfare, market by market. These mechanisms achieve between 91% and 96% of the welfare with optimal pricing. Wait performs best, followed by Rematch, and Deny comes last. All three mechanisms are an important improvement over not doing anything about WGCs (86.9%), but even imperfect surge pricing, as observed in the data, achieves a higher level of welfare (97.3%). We obtain a similar picture if we focus on the welfare of different sides of the market or on WGC markets. The main takeaway is that surge pricing outperforms these mechanisms in avoiding WGCs. One may then wonder whether surge pricing is always better. Online Appendix C.8 presents a detailed market-by-market comparison. Focusing on WGC markets, we find that surge pricing Pareto dominates these three mechanisms in the vast majority of cases. The only exceptions are a few markets in which Wait and Rematch yield marginally higher rider surplus (up to 4%) than surge pricing. This only occurs in markets with mild WGCs, in which a small price increase is enough to avoid the WGC. In markets with strong WGCs, in contrast, surge pricing leads to significantly higher levels of rider surplus, driver surplus, and profits. In fact, these three quantities are often several times higher with surge pricing. In conclusion, although our three protocols can handle mild WGCs reasonably well, more severe WGCs are much better dealt through surge pricing. We undertake a similar comparison for non-WGC markets in Online Appendix C.8. The disparities in welfare are relatively minor: almost always less than 2%. Surge pricing tends to perform better, but there is a notable fraction of markets where Wait and Rematch perform somewhat better. However, these welfare differences are unrelated to WGCs. 6.1.3. Use of These Matching Protocols. Platforms do not purely match with a first-dispatch protocol; they incorporate to some extent our three matching protocols. For instance, DiDi does not use surge pricing in China. Instead, it sets up queues at times and locations of high demand. Queues are a form of waiting that is less efficient than Wait: Instead of matching the newly available driver to the nearest rider, it matches according to the order of trip request. Uber and Lyft use surge pricing as the main tool to avoid WGCs, but to improve the quality of matches they also use the strategies we describe. Uber, for instance, no longer matches riders immediately. Instead, it batches all trips requested during a period of a few seconds and runs an optimization algorithm to determine matches (Yan et al. 2020). Thus, it effectively makes riders wait a few seconds—not enough to induce switches to the competition, but enough to allow for meaningful improvements in matching. A few seconds, however, are not enough to avoid most WGCs. Batches over periods on the order of minutes would be necessary to manage WGCs. Uber and Lyft also use rematching (Brown 2016,Yan et al. 2020). The goal is not to avoid far-away matches, like the ones that result in WGCs; instead, they exploit situations that improve the overall efficiency of the matching algorithm. Thus, rather than fixing a matching failure, they make a well-functioning market work even better by exploiting drivers who drop off a passenger close a rider who is currently matched. Uber also has successfully used Deny in combination with surge pricing. For the period we observe, Uber fixes a maximum ETA between 10 and 25 minutes (which roughly corresponds to a MDR between 2.5 and 6 km) that varies by location but not over time—as would be necessary if it was the main tool to manage WGCs. We show in Online Appendix C.9 that settingR= 2.5 km dominates first dispatch: denying trips works as a circuit breaker that limits the sharp decrease in welfare during WGCs, and it does not affect behavior when the market is working well. A more aggressive MDR of around 1 km would be better because it would further attenuate the impact of WGCs. Rows 7–9 inTable 2present the welfare benefits that can arise from using Deny, Wait, and Rematch as a circuit breaker. Prices are set as in the status quo, and the MDR is set atR= 750 m. In all three cases, we see modest benefits in WGC markets (between 1% and 2% of welfare), but the benefits are almost negligible for the market as a whole. 6.2. Chained and Pooled Trips One adjustment to the matching protocol that increases the efficiency of matching is to match some riders to drivers who are about to drop off a passenger. This effectively increases the density of idle drivers and reduces pickup times. In Online Appendix C.5, we show that this makes matching more efficient, and it could potentially avoid WGCs but not in the circumstances found in our data. For this protocol to avoid WGCs, the market would have to be at least three times larger in terms of the number of trips, or, equivalently, drivers would have to be at least three times as willing to accept trips before dropping off a passenger than they are according toCastillo (2023). Another adjustment that may improve efficiency is to pool trips (as with UberPool or Lyft Line). Riders get a cheaper ride by being matched, if possible, to other riders going in the same direction. Driver time is used more efficiently, so more trips are supplied by the same number of drivers. However, we show in Online Appendix C.6 that the trip supply curve is still backward bending, so WGCs take place when demand is high relative to supply.
article.7. Conclusion: Ride-hailing apps have improved transportation through market design innovations in matching and pricing. Whereas public opinion mostly supports these novelties in matching, consumers have mixed feelings about surge pricing, especially because of worries that it is a form of price gouging. We argued that matching and pricing are tied together in important ways: ride-hailing platforms like Uber and Lyft use a matching algorithm that performs better than traditional street-hail matching but only under the right pricing. Otherwise, the market enters WGCs, a market failure in which drivers spend too much time picking up far-away customers. Other platforms, like DiDi, have chosen to avoid surge pricing and the consumer backlash that comes with it, but they are forced to make possibly undesirable adjustments to the matching protocol. Future research about ride-hailing markets could follow many interesting directions. One policy-relevant dimension is competition between platforms and its interaction with surge pricing. Our analysis highlights two surprising ways in which competition might be undesirable. First, if idle drivers open competing applications simultaneously, they become a common pool resource. If one platform rations drivers, these drivers may be diverted to other platforms, which destroys the first platform’s ability and incentive to manage WGCs.21Second, as ride-hailing platforms grow to be dominant on the roads, a monopolist has an incentive to manage road congestion, whereas competitors do not. These issues suggest that utility regulation may be more appropriate in correcting the harms of market power than competition policy.
